There’s some really interesting articles from people like Jancis Robinson talking about how rapidly they fell out of favor, and you know, some of that was their own fault, and I think a lot of that was also just market conditions, and due to the American markets falling too. It was, it was a number of things, it was complex, but they, you know, then found themselves in a wine glut with too much wine, because then they had all these vines in the ground, and of course, wine, it’s a one crop a year deal, you know, you get one one crop per year, and it also takes about five years for when you plant from when you plant a vineyard for fruit to even come onto a vine. So the industry of wine moves much slower, like to make to grow and make wine moves at a much slower pace than the economy, or then trends, you know, move because trends people can turn on and off much quicker to a variety or style or country or region than the actual industry can keep up with, so that’s always been a challenge. Not just for Aussie wine, but wines all over, and we are currently in one of those, one of those – I hate to say bust, like boom and bust, but in one of those downturns at the moment, where there is just too much wine for what the market can handle, and then that creates just a really tough situation for growers, of course, it’s often the growers who are the ones that suffer the most at the hands of these, you know, bigger corporate companies who aren’t just aren’t able to absorb that fruit anymore, they’re not buying it, the price of fruit goes way down.

So I guess that’s all touching on the economic side that has been challenging. On the climate change side, you know, Australia, it’s no mystery has has had some really tough vintages again, emphasizing that Australia is a very big country, so what’s happening in one region might be totally different from what’s happening in another region, but you know, just earlier this year there were some terrible bushfires up in some of the mountain ranges in Victoria that were just devastating. The Hunter Valley, for example, this year, after the wettest winter they’d had in 60 years, went into one of their hottest, driest summers, and had sort of fast and furious pick there. There’s regions in the Riverland that have reached like 121 degrees over the summer, so they’ve got some extremes the way that we’re seeing all over the world, right, extreme heat, but then it’s not been all hot all the time. There was the between 2021-2023 brought cooler, wetter weather, La Nina influence to South Australia and Victoria, and then flooding became an issue, and there’s places where frost, like in the Clare Valley, is a perfect example of where frost is increasingly becoming an issue, so these again are not unique to Australia. They are happening literally all over the world. 

There is no corner of the wine world that has not been touched by climate change in some regard or another, but I would venture to say Australia has had it quite tough in recent years. But on the flip side of that, as I mentioned, with the AWRI, the Australian Wine Research Institute, they also, because of that, have some of the most innovative research and technology, and they’re doing some really amazing stuff on the viticulture side to try and mitigate and adapt to this changing climate, so, and also, you know, because of Australia’s just economically speaking again, because of this boom and bust history that they have, they’re pretty resilient. I think whenever they have tough times, they emerge better as a region, you know. So, yes, there are bound to be some casualties, there are going to be wineries that shutter, they are going to be vineyards that are ripped up, which is, of course, terrible and a tragedy, but also what my hope is that despite all of those challenges, they will emerge with an even more resilient wine scene, with even better wines from grapes being grown from regions that should be growing them in the soil, they should be growing them in the climate, they should be growing up and growing them in a sustainable way. In general, I think Australia is really leaders on the viticulture front, leaders on the labor labor front … So I hope that that will just continue, and that out of, as they say, out of these challenges, they will emerge even better than before.
